speaker: interviewee
text: After I finished my training in Denver, I took two and a half months off with a friend, traveled through Europe, visited my relatives in Israel, visited with my foster family in Switzerland, and went on a trip through France and saw some of the places where I had been-- [INAUDIBLE]. I went to Gurs. All I found there was a fence in huge area, with huge signs on 'em-- restricted area due to contamination. There was no memorial there yet. And then we spent some time in Italy visiting a school friend of mine there, and also went to Holland and Belgium, and then back to France, and then came back to the United States. And I went back to Denver and found a job working as a restaurant manager in Cheyenne, Wyoming at the Hitching Post Inn for-- I worked there for about two years. Then I went to Bakersfield, lived there for two years, worked as catering manager at The Party Center for the Mossman family. After that, I came to Los Angeles and started working in restaurants here. And during this period, I also met Susie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Susie.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a cousin of my mother's, who was working in-- in-- in Berkeley. And she was coordinating nursing services for the state of California. She came to Bakersfield while I was there visiting. And she told me that I should be seeing more people and get-- think about marriage and so forth. I said, there's no Jewish girls around here. They go to college. They leave Bakersfield and they never come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few weeks later, she called me and she says she has friends who know a girl that might be interested in meeting you. Would I be interested? So I said yes. So she checked it out and sent me a phone number, and address, and a name, which was Susie. And I called her once when I was going to San Francisco for a weekend. And she was busy going skiing, so I didn't see her then. Then, when I moved to Los Angeles, my company was sending me for a training session for four weeks to San Francisco. And so I called her again. And she says, well, are you still in Bakersfield? I said, no. I'm in Los Angeles now. And I'm coming to San Francisco for a week. And she consented to see me. So we made a date. And I went to her-- I rented a car, went to the house. And we-- I met her. We went to dinner at the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] El [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Mirador Hotel in Sausalito, a very beautiful place. And we had a nice evening, except I was doing all the talking, and she couldn't figure out who I was talking about with all this history.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back home. I took her home and she asked me inside to meet her mother. And it turns out that mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fell in love with me before Susie did. And mother invited me for the next evening for dinner. It was Academy Awards night, and we were gonna watch the Academy Awards. We had a great time. And I liked her mother immediately and always got along well with her. I went back to LA, and then we started corresponding. And Susie started to come down on weekends. And pretty soon, it was a weekend for her down once a month, and the weekend up in the Bay Area for me once a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finally get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- we got engaged the end of that summer, 29th of September, 1962. We got married July 7, 1963. And we couldn't get married before then, before-- because Susie had a contract with the Albany school district to teach until the end of that school year. And then we moved-- she moved down here. And we started living in an apartment in the [? Wilshire ?] district first. And then in 1964, we bought this house, where we've been ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] We have two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] Steven is 29. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Andy is 27 years old. They're both here. And you'll meet them later. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm very proud of both of them, and very proud of Susie. She's a wonderful wife and mother. Last September, we had-- last November, we had a very sad event. Mother and a cousin of Susie's died in an accident-- automobile accident. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's been very difficult since, for all of us. But we're working through it and managing to get over the shock and the sadness. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Louis was only 60 years old, and just retiring and starting another life. Mother was 86 years old and in good health. She had some sort of a seizure while driving him to the BART station. And they ran into a tree. She never recovered consciousness. And he died a couple weeks later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The focus of our family has been in getting all the things taken care up there and taking care of things here. I still work. I'm working for Duro Test Corporation-- have been working for-- for them since 1972, when I quit the restaurant business because it was too demanding and not enough income. And I have been working for Duro Test as a sales rep ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gunther, what has your religious life been like since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have always attended a Conservative synagogue. And here, we're members of Adat Ari El in North Hollywood-- have been since 1964, '65, the first holiday after we moved into the house here. Susie is quite active in the sisterhood. I'm not as active. I'm not a committee man. I hate the bickering that goes on in these meetings. And-- but we attend regular services, and the boys do too.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was one thing you can say, a message that you could deliver to your sons, or future grandchildren, or viewers, anybody who might be watching your testimony, what would you say about your experiences during the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] Never forget what happened. Never forget the suffering and the killing that occurred. And never, never [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look at anyone who is not Jewish and think it couldn't happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You never know where the next anti-semitic action comes from. And as my grandmother-- my mother-in-law used to say, she never went back to Austria because she said, I wouldn't know which face to spit into. And I feel that no matter how sweet and wonderful it is here, it can happen again with the right kind of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] initiative, whether it's a Robertson, or a McCarthy, or anyone else. And we can't forget. It's the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Gunther. Can you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my wife, Susie. We've been married since 1963. Behind Susie is Steven. He's 29 years old. And behind me is Andy, who is 27 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm just so pleased that Gunther did this interview today because I know it's something that he's been wanting to do. And I'm glad now that it's behind him because I know it was not easy for him to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd like to say something. I just wanted to thank the Neff family. I'd like to speak to-- thank the Neff family in Switzerland, especially, for encouraging my father to document his story, and also for encouraging him to continue his Jewish education in-- when in Switzerland. It's had a-- a very strong effect on the whole family, and obviously has made him be here with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just want to say thank you for dad to do this. And-- and hopefully, he will continue to keep updating his records, And he's already started with those, and continue with the writings that he's also done. And he keeps remembering new things, so hopefully he'll continue to do that and have it documented even more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a little closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my father, Sally Katz, my mother, Trude Katz, and myself, taken late '38 or early '39 after my father had recovered from his ordeal at Dachau Concentration Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Gunther Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in a photo studio in Speyer, Germany. [BACKROUND NOISE] This is a photograph of my family in Speyer. We're standing in the back-- in the yard of the house where we lived. The people in the picture-- front, left-- myself, Gunther Katz; next to me, my cousin Lizel Kling. Starting from the left, the adults-- my grandfather Elias Kling. Behind him, just ahead of my grandmother, Mina Kling. Next to her, my uncle Julius Meyer and his wife next to him, Hadie Meyer. Next to her is my mother, Trude Katz. Next to her, Ruth Kling, Lizel's mother, and her father, Ernst Kling. It was taken Easter 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Speyer, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself, Gunther Katz, taken in front of our house at 26 [INAUDIBLE] in Speyer, probably around the time of my first day of school in 1936, in the spring. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a photograph of my foster family and myself in Winterthur, Switzerland in September-- or October 1945. Left to right, near the bottom front, is my foster brother, [? Ferdi, ?] who was at about three and a half years old. I am behind him. Next to him is my foster mother, Helen Neff; their baby, my foster sister, [? Martali, ?] who was born in September of that year. Next to her is my foster father, Jacob Neff. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my foster parents, now known as Mueti and Atti, Helen Neff and Jacob Neff, in Winterthur, in front of their house, I would say, in the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Switzerland. This is a picture of Susie and myself-- Susie, my wife-- at our wedding, July 7th 1963 at the Beth El Temple in Berkeley, California. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of Chateau de [INAUDIBLE] in France, Provence [INAUDIBLE]. It was taken in 1958, when I returned there for a visit. I have been there again since. It is the place where I was on vacation in 1941 in the summer, and returned in 1942, after my parents were deported. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a postcard that my uncle Ernst Kling sent from somewhere in Silesia, where he was in prison. We suppose that he was working in a mine or a factory of some sort. It was sent to our contact in Switzerland, who forwarded to us. He said in the photograph that-- in the postcard that he was separated from his wife and asked that I take care of his daughter, Lizel, and he would write again. But we never heard from him again. This was mailed in December of 1943.
